stem: How many positive divisors does \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \) have?
solution: If \( n = p^{a} q^{b} \) then the number of positive divisors is \( (a+1)(b+1) = ([[a]]+1)([[b]]+1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the exponents; each exponent must be increased by one first.
This forgets to count 1 (or \( n \) itself) as a divisor.
This adds the factor counts instead of multiplying them.
This multiplies the exponents and adjusts by a constant.
This overcounts the divisors by two.
This adds the exponents rather than using \( (a+1)(b+1) \).

stem: How many positive divisors does the number \( [[nval]] \) have?
solution: First factor \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \); the number of positive divisors is then \( ([[a]]+1)([[b]]+1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( n \) itself, not its divisor count.
This adds the exponents directly, without factoring or the +1 shift.
This forgets to count \( n \) itself among its divisors.
This adds the factor counts instead of multiplying them.
This is one more than the correct divisor count.
This is one less than the correct divisor count.

stem: Let \( n = [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \). How many positive divisors does \( n^{2} = [[n2val]] \) have?
solution: Squaring \( n \) doubles every exponent: \( n^{2} = [[p]]^{2\cdot[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{2\cdot[[b]]} \), so its divisor count is \( (2\cdot[[a]]+1)(2\cdot[[b]]+1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the divisor count of \( n \) itself, forgetting that squaring doubles the exponents.
This doubles the divisor count of \( n \) instead of doubling the exponents first.
This adds the doubled-exponent terms instead of multiplying them.
This quadruples the divisor count of \( n \), overcorrecting for the square.
This is one more than the correct divisor count.
This is one less than the correct divisor count.

stem: Let \( n = [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \). How many positive divisors of \( n \) are divisible by \( [[q]] \)?
solution: A divisor of \( n \) divisible by \( [[q]] \) has the form \( [[p]]^{i}[[q]]^{j} \) with \( 0\le i\le[[a]] \) and \( 1\le j\le[[b]] \), giving \( ([[a]]+1)\cdot[[b]] = [[answer]] \) such divisors.
distractor_rationales: This is the divisor count of all of \( n \), not just the ones divisible by \( [[q]] \).
This only counts the allowed exponents of \( [[q]] \), forgetting to multiply by the choices for \( [[p]] \)'s exponent.
This drops one too many exponent choices for \( [[q]] \).
This multiplies the raw exponents instead of the number of allowed exponent choices.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.

stem: How many positive divisors does \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \cdot [[r]]^{[[c]]} \) have?
solution: For \( n = p^{a}q^{b}r^{c} \) with three distinct primes, the number of positive divisors multiplies across all three prime factors: \( (a+1)(b+1)(c+1) = ([[a]]+1)([[b]]+1)([[c]]+1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the raw exponents instead of increasing each by one first.
This forgets to count 1 (or \( n \) itself) as a divisor.
This adds the three shifted exponent counts instead of multiplying them.
This drops the third prime factor \( [[r]] \) entirely, using only two of the three factors.
This is one more than the correct divisor count.
This is one less than the correct divisor count.
This is two more than the correct divisor count.

stem: Let \( n = [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \). How many positive divisors of \( n \) are PERFECT SQUARES?
solution: A divisor \( p^{i}q^{j} \) of \( n \) is a perfect square exactly when both \( i \) and \( j \) are even, with \( 0 \le i \le [[a]] \) and \( 0 \le j \le [[b]] \). The even choices for \( i \) number \( \lfloor [[a]]/2 \rfloor + 1 \), and likewise \( \lfloor [[b]]/2 \rfloor + 1 \) for \( j \), giving \( (\lfloor [[a]]/2\rfloor+1)(\lfloor [[b]]/2\rfloor+1) = [[answer]] \) square divisors.
distractor_rationales: This is the total divisor count of \( n \), not just the perfect-square divisors.
This forgets to count the exponent 0 (i.e. dividing by 1) as an even choice.
This adds the two even-exponent counts instead of multiplying them.
This rounds the exponents up instead of down before halving, overcounting the even choices.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.
This is two more than the correct count.
